description: In June 2019, two oil tankers were attacked in the Gulf of Oman near the Strait of Hormuz in incidents widely attributed to Iran. The attacks prompted a 20-25% temporary drop in tanker transit counts and a sharp spike in war-risk insurance premiums. US Naval forces were deployed to the region, significantly elevating geopolitical tension and triggering coordinated international monitoring.
impact: Temporary halt of tanker transit through Gulf of Oman, spike in marine war-risk insurance rates by 10x, oil prices rose 4-5%, heightened US-Iran military tension in the Strait.
lessons: Geopolitical attacks on key maritime chokepoints cause rapid insurance repricing and traffic reduction before any physical supply impact materialises; early news sentiment signals precede vessel-count drops by 2-3 days.

description: On 23 March 2021 the container ship Ever Given ran aground in the Suez Canal and blocked the waterway for six days, halting approximately $9.6 billion of daily trade. Over 369 ships queued at both ends of the canal. The blockage triggered immediate rerouting of vessels via the Cape of Good Hope, adding 10-14 days to voyages.
impact: 369 vessels queued, $9.6B/day trade halted, container freight rates rose 15%, Cape of Good Hope traffic surged, global just-in-time supply chains disrupted for weeks after refloating.
lessons: A single vessel grounding at a critical chokepoint can trigger cascading global rerouting; routing anomaly detection would have flagged the traffic cessation within hours of the grounding.

description: From October 2023, Houthi militants launched sustained missile and drone attacks on commercial vessels transiting the Red Sea in solidarity with Gaza, causing a 55% reduction in Red Sea shipping traffic at peak disruption. Major carriers including MSC, Maersk, and CMA CGM suspended Red Sea transits and rerouted via the Cape of Good Hope. Freight rates doubled within weeks.
impact: Red Sea container traffic fell 55%, Suez Canal revenues dropped 40%, Cape of Good Hope transits surged 200%, global container freight rates doubled, transit time Asia-Europe increased 10-14 days.
lessons: Sustained geopolitical attack campaigns against shipping chokepoints create self-reinforcing rerouting loops; news sentiment signals and rerouting percentage both lead vessel-count drops by 5-7 days.

event: Iran Hormuz Mine Threat and Naval Exercises
description: In mid-2010, Iran conducted large-scale naval exercises in the Strait of Hormuz and explicitly threatened to mine the strait in response to expanding US and EU sanctions. While no actual mining occurred, the credible threat triggered temporary traffic reduction and insurance premium spikes. US Fifth Fleet and allied navies increased patrols, further elevating tension.
impact: Modest 8% traffic reduction, war-risk insurance premiums rose 20%, oil spot prices elevated, allied naval presence in the strait doubled temporarily.
lessons: Credible mine-threat scenarios at Hormuz have an outsized market impact relative to actual physical disruption; geopolitical signals lead shipping signals by 3-5 days.

description: 2011 represented the peak year of Somali piracy with 237 vessel attacks, 28 hijackings, and over 1,000 seafarers held hostage. Piracy extended across a 2.5 million square-mile zone of the Indian Ocean. Many vessels rerouted via the Cape of Good Hope to avoid the high-risk corridor, and others paid for armed naval escorts through the internationally recommended transit corridor.
impact: Shipping industry cost $7B annually in rerouting, security, and ransoms; 28 vessels hijacked with 1,063 hostages at peak; transit times through Gulf of Aden increased 40%.
lessons: Persistent low-intensity security threats cause structural rerouting shifts rather than acute disruptions; vessel-count and routing anomalies are the primary leading indicators.

event: Japan Tohoku Earthquake and Port Closures
description: The magnitude 9.0 Tohoku earthquake and resulting tsunami on 11 March 2011 destroyed or severely damaged multiple Japanese ports including Sendai, Ishinomaki, and Hachinohe. The disasters triggered immediate closure of automotive and electronics supply chains, with Japan's manufacturing output falling 20% in March. Radiation concerns at Fukushima further disrupted maritime traffic in the affected region.
impact: Sendai, Ishinomaki, Hachinohe ports closed for weeks; Japan manufacturing output fell 20%; global automotive supply chains halted; radiation exclusion zones disrupted 15% of Japan Pacific traffic.
lessons: Earthquake and tsunami events cause near-instantaneous port closures with weeks-long recovery; natural disaster severity and proximity scores are the earliest signals, preceding vessel-count drops by 0-6 hours.

description: Throughout 2021, pandemic-driven demand surges and labour shortages caused catastrophic port congestion globally. At peak in October 2021, over 90 container ships sat at anchor off Los Angeles and Long Beach awaiting berths. Container shipping rates surged 400-500% above pre-pandemic levels. The simultaneous congestion at multiple major hubs created a systemic freight market shock.
impact: 90+ ships at anchor off LA/Long Beach, container spot rates up 400%, average transit time extended by 3-4 weeks, retail supply chains impacted globally, inflation accelerated.
lessons: Demand-driven port congestion is signalled by congestion index and freight rate increases simultaneously; shipping anomaly detection combined with market signals provides 2-3 week advance warning of systemic disruption.

event: US West Coast Port Labour Dispute
region: US West Coast Ports (LA, Long Beach, Seattle)
description: A contract dispute between the International Longshore and Warehouse Union (ILWU) and Pacific Maritime Association from November 2014 to February 2015 caused a deliberate work-to-rule slowdown at 29 major West Coast ports. Productivity fell 50-60%, creating massive cargo backlogs. Some importers shifted cargo to East Coast ports, adding weeks of transit time.
impact: 29 ports affected, cargo backlogs of 20-30 vessels per port, US agricultural exporters lost $2.5B, retailers diverted cargo to East Coast adding 2-3 weeks transit time.
lessons: Labour-dispute-driven port slowdowns produce gradual vessel-count and congestion anomaly build-up over days rather than acute spikes; market freight-rate signals confirm when the slowdown reaches systemic levels.

description: The European Union oil embargo effective 1 July 2012, combined with strengthened US financial sanctions, caused Iranian oil exports to fall from 2.5 million to approximately 1.0 million barrels per day. Iran responded with repeated threats to close the Strait of Hormuz. Brent crude prices remained elevated above $100/barrel throughout the period and shipping insurers classified the Hormuz as a war-risk zone.
impact: Iran's oil exports fell 60%, Brent crude averaged $111/barrel throughout 2012, Hormuz war-risk insurance premiums spiked, Iranian tanker fleet became effectively stranded due to insurance blacklisting.
lessons: Economic sanctions targeting an oil-producing nation at a critical chokepoint drive sustained oil-price elevation rather than acute spikes; geopolitical and market signals co-move with a 1-2 week lag.

event: Cyclone Gonu Oman and Gulf of Oman Disruption
description: Cyclone Gonu, a Category 5 storm and the strongest tropical cyclone ever recorded in the Arabian Sea, struck Oman on 6 June 2007 before weakening into the Gulf of Oman. The cyclone caused the closure of the port of Sohar, disrupted tanker traffic in the Gulf of Oman approach to the Strait of Hormuz, and forced vessels to hold position for 3-4 days. Oman's oil infrastructure sustained moderate damage.
impact: Sohar and Salalah ports closed 3-4 days, tanker traffic in Gulf of Oman halted, Oman LNG exports disrupted, oil prices spiked modestly, vessels holding positions outside the Gulf of Oman approach.
lessons: Tropical cyclone events in the Arabian Sea create acute short-duration routing disruptions detectable 24-48 hours in advance via weather tracking; cyclone severity and vessel holding anomalies are the key leading indicators.
